coredata.dc:title: L-cysteine modified N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from peach palm (Bactris gasipaes) peels for detection of mercury ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gómez Pineros, Brayan Stiven
Granados-Oliveros, Gilma
coredata.dc:description: Establishing an effective strategy for Hg2+ determination with good sensitivity and high selectivity is of particular concern. In this work, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were obtained for the first time from peels of peach palm fruit (Bactris gasipaes) through microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis. Surface NCQDs was modified with l-cysteine (NCQDs/CYS) by a chemical reaction (known as amide coupling) between the nitrogen-containing groups (amine group) of l-cysteine ligand and the carboxyl groups of NCQDs. The chemical bonding was examined by Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). NCQDs/CYS nanoparticles exhibited a quantum yield of fluorescence (ΦFL) of 25.4%, high solubility in water, and a surface with thiol groups from l-cysteine. Mercury ions induced quenching of fluorescence of NCQDs/CYS at low concentrations (Limit of Detection, LOD = 0.19 μM) with great sensitivity. A significant preference for Hg2+ ions was found because of the high affinity between the thiolate groups of NCQDs/CYS and the analyte. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were done to study the way that led to fluorescence quenching when Hg2+ ions were present, evidencing a static quenching fluorescence mechanism. The new NCQDs/CYS material could be applied to accurately measure the concentration of Hg2+ ions in water samples in a fast and cost-effective way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peach palm
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Mercury ion
Static quenching

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Nuevos Materiales Nano y Supramoleculares research group at the Department of Chemistry, Universidad Nacional de Colombia, and Professeur Sebastian Bello of Photochemistry Laboratory, Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro for Time-resolved fluorescence measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the simplified structure of NCQDs/CYS.
X-ray diffraction patterns of NCQDs, and NCQDs/CYS.
(A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of NCQDs and (B) size distribution of NCQDs. (C) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of NCQDs/CYS and (D) size distribution of NCQDs/CYS.
FT-IR spectra of (A) NCQDs (B) l-cysteine and (C) NCQDs/CYS.
Full XPS spectrum of (A) NCQDs and (B) NCQDs/CYS.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (A) C1s and (B) N1s of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (A) O1s for NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS, and (B) S2p of NCQDs/CYS.
Zeta potential distributions of NCQDs (red line) and NCQDs/CYS (blue line).

(A) UV–Vis (black lines) and fluorescence spectra (red lines) excited at 360 nm. (B) Fluorescence spectra were excited at different wavelengths (λex = 340 – 440 nm). (C) Digital images of an aqueous solution of NCQDs/CYS under sunlight (left) and UV irradiation (right).

(A) Fluorescence spectrum intensity of NCQDs/CYS at different concentrations of Hg2+ from 5 to 35 μM. (B) Stern-Volmer plot between the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs/CYS and the concentration of Hg2+. (C) Fluorescence intensity of NCQDs/CYS after adding different metal ions.
Photo of an aqueous solution of NCQDs/CYS before and after adding Hg2+ ions.
(A) Fluorescence decay curves. (B) Absorption spectra. (C) Normalized FT-IR of NCQDs/CYS and NCQDs/CYS + Hg2+.
Simplified scheme of the Hg2+ detection mechanism by NCQDs/CYS.

head.ce:title: L-cysteine modified N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from peach palm (Bactris gasipaes) peels for detection of mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Grupo de Síntesis Orgánica Sostenible, Departamento de Química, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Nacional de Colombia, Bogotá, D. C., Colombia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Grupo de Síntesis Orgánica Sostenible, Departamento de Química, Facultad de Ciencias

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Grupo de Síntesis Orgánica Sostenible, Departamento de Química, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Nacional de Colombia, Bogotá, D. C., Colombia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Establishing an effective strategy for Hg2+ determination with good sensitivity and high selectivity is of particular concern. In this work, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were obtained for the first time from peels of peach palm fruit (Bactris gasipaes) through microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis. Surface NCQDs was modified with l-cysteine (NCQDs/CYS) by a chemical reaction (known as amide coupling) between the nitrogen-containing groups (amine group) of l-cysteine ligand and the carboxyl groups of NCQDs. The chemical bonding was examined by Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). NCQDs/CYS nanoparticles exhibited a quantum yield of fluorescence (ΦFL) of 25.4%, high solubility in water, and a surface with thiol groups from l-cysteine. Mercury ions induced quenching of fluorescence of NCQDs/CYS at low concentrations (Limit of Detection, LOD = 0.19 μM) with great sensitivity. A significant preference for Hg2+ ions was found because of the high affinity between the thiolate groups of NCQDs/CYS and the analyte. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were done to study the way that led to fluorescence quenching when Hg2+ ions were present, evidencing a static quenching fluorescence mechanism. The new NCQDs/CYS material could be applied to accurately measure the concentration of Hg2+ ions in water samples in a fast and cost-effective way.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is highly toxic and is known as the most potent neurotoxin in human physiology. Hg2+ ions, perhaps the most lethal form of mercury, have been related to several central nervous system diseases and are intimately linked to environmental pollution [1–3]. Mercury detection through traditional methods (for example, mass spectrometry and atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy) requires sophisticated and very high-cost equipment, and sample preparation tends to be complex. Developing heavy metal optical sensors is a strategy for fast and low analysis costs, and they do not require difficult-to-access equipment [4–6]. Different optical sensors have been applied to detect metal ions, among which organic dyes, inorganic nanoparticles, and quantum dots stand out [6–8]. These materials have disadvantages since they are prepared from complex synthesis routes involving expensive or toxic precursors. In addition, some optical sensors may have low stability in environmental conditions since they are susceptible to aggregation and photooxidation in aqueous medium, affecting their optical properties [9,10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoparticles that have generated significant attention among researchers, given their properties and applications [11]. CQDs have a spherical structure with sizes of less than 10 nm and stand out for their excellent solubility in water, low toxicity, and favorable biocompatibility. In addition, they are characterized by their strong absorption and fluorescence in the visible region [12]. CQDs can be doped with nitrogen (NCQDs) to improve their optical properties [13,14] and to introduce functional groups (such as -NH2), producing an effect on selectivity in metal sensing applications [15]. This aspect is particularly relevant in fluorescent detection of Hg2+ [15,16]. For instance, Liu et al. [17] applied NCQDs (synthesized from citric acid and ammonium hydroxide) in the selective detection of Hg2+ ions. They proposed a mechanism mediated by the affinity of Hg2+ ions to the -NH2, -OH, and -COOH groups of NCQDs. N- and S-doped carbon dots (prepared from 3-mercaptopropionic acid and different amines) were used to detect Hg2+ ions selectively [18]. NCQDs obtained from strawberry juice with a high nitrogen content and a surface composed of -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups achieved the selective detection of Hg2+ ions by quenching of fluorescence [19].

CQDs have shown remarkable performance in detecting Hg2+ in terms of LOD (nM range) and sensitivity [15,16]. Preparation from natural sources is an economical alternative and is more advantageous for the environment [20,21]. However, the selectivity and specificity of CQDs and NCQDs to metal ions have not been fully understood, perhaps due to the diversity of functional groups on their surface. Based on the Pearson acid-base theory [15,22], carbon dots can be superficially modified with strategic molecules to increase affinity and specificity towards an analyte. In this context, we chose l-cysteine to modify the surface of NCQDs. This amino acid is a soft Lewis base with a highly polarizable R-S group and a strong affinity for soft Hg2+ ions. Obtaining NCQDs bound to l-cysteine (NCQDs/CYS) seeks a better interpretation of the selectivity towards mercury ions.

This work reports the first use of biomass derived from peach palm fruit peels for preparing fluorescent NCQDs upon microwave irradiation. Functionalization of NCQDs with l-cysteine (NCQDs/CYS) was done through a coupling reaction amide-type of NCQD's carboxylic groups and the amine group of l-cysteine. NCQDs/CYS were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), FT-IR, XPS, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopies. Changes in the structural and optical properties of NCQDs/CYS (contrasted with NCQDs) revealed a route to bind covalently biomass-derived carbon dots to an amino acid. Fig. 1 displays a simplified structure of NCQDs/CYS.

Quenching of fluorescence of NCQDs/CYS induced by Hg2+ in aqueous media was evaluated. Results evidenced the high efficiency of NCQDs/CYS to detect selectively Hg2+, with a sensitivity of 4.10×104 M −1 and LOD of 0.19 μM. A possible mechanism was established based on fluorescence decay measurements and Pearson's theory. Results showed that forming a nonfluorescent coordination complex between the mercury ions (soft acid) and the thiol group (soft base) of NCQDs/CYS produced a static quenching mechanism.

L-cysteine, ethylenediamine, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), HgCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, PbCl2, ZnCl2, MnCl2, CdCl2, NaAsO2, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich.

Peach palm (Bactris gasipaes) peels (250 g) were collected from a central market in Bogotá, D. C., Colombia. The peels were washed using distilled water and cut into small pieces. After this, the peels were dried by dialysis to obtain a homogeneous powder.

NCQDs synthesis was done using the following procedure: 1.8 g of peach palm (Bactris gasipaes) peels (dry powders), 4.5 mL of ethylenediamine (67.0 mmol), and 15.5 mL of distilled water were placed in a borosilicate reactor, which was introduced in an Anton Paar microwave reactor (monowave 200). The reaction was left at 170 °C under magnetic stirring for 2 h [6,23]. NCQDs purification was performed by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove larger particles and filtered with 0.45 μm microfilters to remove the smaller particles. A dialysis membrane with a pore size of 3.5 KD was used for 12 h to remove the unreacted precursors. Finally, NCQDs were lyophilized for 24 h until dryness, obtaining NCQDs powders.
Carbon dots without N-doping were synthesized and purified using the procedures employed to obtain NCQDs, but no ethylenediamine was used during the experimentation.

L-cysteine-modified NCQDs were prepared using the following procedure: 300 mg of NCQDs were mixed with a solution composed of 300 mg (2.4 mmols) of EDC, 30 mg (0.3 mmols) of NHS, and 60 mL of water under magnetic stirring for 4 h at room temperature. 100 mg (0.8 mmols) of l-cysteine was added to 10 mL of a phosphate buffer (pH=7). This solution is mixed with the EDC/NHS solution and left to react for 4 h [20,24]. NCQDs/CYS were purified following the described procedure for the NCQDs sample.

X-ray diffraction measurements were carried out on a PAnalytical X'Pert3 Multipurpose Diffractometer, with X-ray generation tubes with Cu (copper), Mo (molybdenum), and Fe (iron) anodes, operating at 45 kV and 40 mA with a λ = Cu- Kα. (1.54 Å).

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) images were taken using a Tecnai F20 Super Twin TMP microscope, field emission source, resolution of 0.1 nm at 200 Kv, maximum magnification in TEM 1.0 MX, GATAN US 1000XP-P camera.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in KBr pellets and recorded between 4000 and 400 cm−1 on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 spectrometer provided by a fast-recovery deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector. The resolution was 4 cm−1, and the number of scans was 4.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using the SPECS (Germany) built XPS/ISS/UPS-ACenteno platform, equipped with a PHOIBOS 150 2D-DLD energy analyzer. A monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (FOCUS 500) operated at 100 W was used for the measurements. The CasaXPS program and the SPECS Prodigy library for RSF values were used for data analysis. The Zeta potential was measured on the Horiba SZ-100 equipment.

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer EMC-11-UV was employed to measure the UV–Vis absorption spectra. A Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer, with a xenon lamp as the excitation source, was employed to measure fluorescence spectra. Fluorescence quantum yield (ΦFL) was determined with Eq. (1).
(1)
Φ
X
=
Φ
S
(
A S
A x
)
(
I X
I S
)
(
n X 2
n S 2
)

S and x correspond to values of standard and sample solutions, respectively. A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I is the area under the emission peak, and n is the refractive index of the solvent [25]. Quinine sulfate dissolved in sulfuric acid (0.05 M) was used as a standard (ΦFL = 0.546, λex = 360 nm).

ALBA Time-Correlated Single Photon Counter (TCSPC) system was used to measure time-resolved fluorescence. A 340 nm laser diode was employed as the excitation source, which emitted pulses of light with a width of 60 ps and a peak power of 300 mW.

The fluorescence quenching of NCQDs/CYS sample induced by Hg2+ ions at various concentrations (0 to 35 μM) was studied following this procedure: an aqueous solution of Hg2+ ions was added to an aqueous solution of NCQDs/CYS (50 mg/L), with a final volume of 3 mL. The mixture was stirred and left in darkness for 10 min at room temperature, and then the fluorescence spectrum (λex = 360 nm) was measured.

The metal ions (Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, and As3+) were studied as possible interferents. In a typical experiment, a mixture composed of an aqueous solution of NCQDs/CYS (50 mg/L) and an aqueous solution of metal ions (35 μM) was prepared, with a final volume of 3 mL. The mixture was stirred and left in darkness for 10 min at room temperature, and then the fluorescence spectrum (λex = 360 nm) was measured.

A simple and direct microwave-assisted synthesis of NCQDs from lyophilized peach palm peels as a carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen-doping precursor was employed. A mixture of these carbon and nitrogen precursors was heated at 170 °C under microwave irradiation for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, peach palm peels-based NCQDs were centrifuged, filtered, and dialyzed in deionized water to obtain purified NCQDs with a fluorescence quantum yield of about 20.5% (calibrated with quinine sulfate as the reference) and the highest mass yield of CDQs (30 %). Microwave treatment of 30 min or 60 min of B. gasipaes peels produced white precipitates, soluble in water, and no fluorescents. These impurities significantly decreased NCQDs' mass yield (less than 8 %). Other investigations using peels, leaves, or grains as CQDs precursors required solvent extractions or pretreatments with concentrated acids or alkalis before short-time microwave treatment [26–28]. The prolonged microwave treatment avoided a pretreatment of peach palm peels, resulting in an environmentally friendly methodology.

The resulting NCQDs were superficially modified with l-cysteine by a coupling reaction amide-type between the amine group of l-cysteine ligand and the carboxyl groups of NCQDs. Structural, morphological, size, and optical characterizations were carried out by XRD, HR-TEM, FT-IR, XPS, Zeta potential, UV–Vis, and fluorescence measurements.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS samples. All samples are amorphous, with a broad diffraction peak centered at 2-theta of 21° for NCQDs sample and 22° for NCQDs/CYS sample. The interlayer spacing (d) is 0.40 nm and 0.39 nm, respectively, matching the (002) plane of amorphous graphitic carbon dots [29,30]. Similar results have been reported for biomass-derived carbon dots [31].

TEM and HR-TEM images of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS samples (Figs. 3 A,C) reveal the obtention of particles with spherical shapes and slightly polydisperse size distributions. The average size of NCQDs is 3.28 ± 0.55 nm, and NCQDs/CYS is 3.48 ± 0.66 nm (Figs. 3B,D). The interplanar distance spacing of selected areas in the HR-TEM images, and applying the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) of ImageJ software, is found to be 0.36 nm for NCQDs and 0.35 nm for NCQDs/CYS. These values coincide with the lattice spacing of (002) graphitic carbon plane, confirming the results of the XRD patterns found. No changes in their size, shape, and morphologic characteristics were obtained when the surface modification of NCQDs with l-cysteine was developed.

Fig. 4 compares FT-IR spectra of NCQDs, l-cysteine, and NCQDs/CYS samples. FT-IR spectrum of NCQDs sample (Fig. 4A) shows the existence of -OH (3380 cm−1), CH sp2 or NH (2935 cm−1), CH sp3 (2852 cm−1), C = O (1653 cm−1), CO (1375 cm−1), CO-C (1089 cm−1), CN (1460 cm−1) and C = N (1557 cm−1) [24,32]. Bands associated with oxygenated groups were also identified in the carbon dots sample prepared without ethylenediamine, while no amine bands were identified (Figure S1). These results are evidence of the introduction of nitrogen atoms producing the N-doped carbon quantum dots with a surface composed of -COOH, C = O, -OH, and -NH2 [29,32].

FT-IR spectrum of l-cysteine in Fig. 4B is characterized by the presence of -OH (3382 cm−1), -NH (2935 cm−1), S-H (2570 cm−1), -COO− asymmetric (1600 cm−1), NH3 symmetric bend (1495 cm−1), -COO− symmetric (1398 cm−1), CH stretching (1320, 1220 cm−1), CN (1105 cm−1), S-H bend (1050 cm−1) C-S (610 cm−1) and -NH3 torsion (490 cm−1) [33,34].

The FT-IR spectrum of the NCQDs/CYS sample (Fig. 4C) shows the presence of l-cysteine and the changes produced after the coupling reaction between the -NH2 group of l-cysteine and the COOH groups of NCQDs. The modifications more significative are observed in the COO− asymmetric (1600 cm−1) and symmetric (1398 cm−1) bands and in the NH3 (1495 cm−1) band of l-cysteine, which changed to two new bands at 1638 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 that correspond respectively to NH bending and C = O stretching vibrations of the amide group [25,36]. These changes are evidence of the formation of the amide bonding between NCQDs and the ligand. The bands associated with thiol group of l-cysteine, such as S-H (2560 cm−1) and C-S (585 cm−1), likewise the CN (1150 cm−1) and CH bands (1320 cm−1) of l-cysteine are maintained in the NCQDs/CYS spectrum. The NH/CH (2935 cm−1/2852 cm−1), CN (1460 cm−1), C = O (1398 cm−1), CO (1375 cm−1), CO-C (1050 cm−1) bands derived from NCQDs are also observed in NCQDs/CYS [35,36].

XPS measurements characterized surface composition and chemical groups of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS and the formation of the amide bonding between NCQDs and the l-cysteine, as was found in the FT-IR study. Full-scan XPS spectrum of NCQDs (Fig. 5 A) shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.9 eV, and 531.9 eV, attributed to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively. Full XPS of NCQDs/CYS sample (Fig. 5B) shows the C, N, and O peaks, and the peak at 166.1 eV of sulfur due to modification of NCQDs surface with l-cysteine.

Fig. 6 A shows the C1s region. For NCQDs sample, C1s peak is deconvoluted into four components at 284.2 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.4 and 287.5 eV attributed to C = C bond (characteristic of the graphitic structure) [37–39], CC/CH bonds, CO [38,39]/CN bonds [39], and OC = O [38,40] bonds, respectively, confirming the existence of numerous nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups on the NCQDs surface [41,42]. For NCQDs/CYS sample, the shape of the C1s spectrum shows a noticeable difference concerning the NCQDs sample. The C1s peak was fitted by five peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.6, 286.3 eV, and 287.8 peV attributed to C = C, CC/CH bonds, C-S/CO/CN bonds, NC = O [43] and OC = O [39,44,45] bonds. The appearance of C-S and NC = O in the NCQDs/CYS sample demonstrates the presence of ligand and the formation of a stable amide bond between l-cysteine and NCQDs.

N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 6B) of NCQDs is decomposed into two peaks at 398.5 eV and 399.7 eV, corresponding to the nitrogen atom of graphite-like structure (CN) [42,46] and NH bond [37,38,47]. These bands are due to the introduction of N atoms into the graphitic structure (graphitic-N) and the formation of NH2 group on NCQDs surface. N1s XPS spectrum of NCQDs/CYS was deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.5 eV corresponding to graphitic-N and 400.0 eV associated with NH/C N = O, respectively [37,48,49]. The presence of amide groups confirms the reaction between the carboxylic group from NCQDS and amine groups from l-cysteine.

O1s XPS spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS are shown in Fig. 7 A. O1s spectrum of NCQDs sample is deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.0 eV and 532.6 eV associated with O = C bond [42,49,50] and OC bond [42,44,50]. These bands are due to hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of NCQDs. When NCQDs were superficially modified with l-cysteine, the components' shape and intensities changed, and the binding energy (BE) shifted to 530.6 eV and 532.2, respectively. These variations are attributed to amide chemical bonding between the carboxylic group from NCQDS and amine groups from l-cysteine.

The S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 7B) shows the S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 peaks at 165.4 eV and 166.5 eV, respectively, associated with the thiols groups of l-cysteine [51]. A peak at 170.3 eV is also observed and could be due to oxidized sulfur species (S = O bond) [18].

Fig. 9 A shows the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS samples. Figure S3 shows UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of CQDs sample (prepared without ethylenediamine). The π-π* band attributed to C = C of graphitic carbon is observed at 273 nm for CQDs. This band is slightly red-shifted, and its intensity decreases for NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS samples. The n-π* band at 345 nm of CQDs (due to oxygen functional groups) is also red-shifted for NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS samples, with increased intensity, due to heteroatoms and auxochromes groups (such as amines, amides) of the surface.

Compared to the emission maximum (443 nm) of CQDs (Figure S3), NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS samples evidence a red-shift in the emission maximum at 448 nm and 452 nm. These changes are due to nitrogen doping and subsequent cysteine functionalization, respectively. Fluorescence quantum yield (ΦFL) of the NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS materials are 20.5 % and 25.4 %, compared with 12.2 % for CQDs. The introduction of chromophoric groups (such as amines and amides) increased the molar absorptivity coefficient of the material and could increase ΦFL (Strickler Berg relation) [20,53].

Fig. 9B shows the typical excitation-dependent feature in the PL intensity of NCQDs and NCQDs/CYS, where the emission maximum shifts to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 nm to 440 nm. Figure S4 shows fluorescence spectra excited at different wavelengths of CQDs. This characteristic behavior of carbon quantum dots (multicolor emission) could be due to quantum confinement effects since the particle sizes are polydisperse. Furthermore, introducing heteroatoms and functional groups generates different surface emission trap states [54]. Fig. 9C shows the digital images of an aqueous solution of NCQDs/CYS under sunlight (left) and UV irradiation (right). UV–Vis and fluorescence results of N-doped carbon quantum dots modified with l-cysteine align with those obtained from biomass [55] and small molecules [56].

Considering the characterization results, NCQDs obtained from the biomass of peach palm (Bactris gasipaes) peels absorb and fluorescence considerably in the visible region. XRD, HRTEM, FT-IR, XPS, and zeta potential measurements demonstrate that NCQDs materials have a semi-crystalline graphite core with a surface rich in amino and carboxylic groups and other oxygenated functional groups. An amide chemical bonding between NCQDs and l-cysteine could be characterized and evidenced. NCQDs/CYS surface is mainly composed by amino, carboxylic acid, amide, and thiol functional groups.

The changes in the fluorescence spectra of NCQDs/CYS sample induced by Hg2+ ions were studied in an aqueous solution. Results show that Hg2+ ions could quench the photoluminescence (PL) intensity. First, we presented the progressive fluorescence quenching by increasing Hg2+ concentration (ranging from 0 to 35 μM) for NCQDs/CYS sample (Fig. 10 A). The PL quenching by increasing the Hg2+ concentration is evaluated in terms of Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (2)):
(2)
I o
/ I = 1 +
K sv
[ H
g
2 +
]

[Hg2+] is mercury ions concentration, Io and I are the fluorescence intensities at a constant wavelength in a solution without Hg2+ ions and a solution with [Hg2+], respectively. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer FL quenching constant [57]. A good lineality is obtained with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9998 (Fig. 10B). The sensitivity (Ksv) is 4.10×104 M −1, and LOD is 0.196 μM (LOD = 3Ϭ/sensitivity, where Ϭ is the standard deviation of the blank [58]).

Some investigations have shown excellent results in Hg2+sensing by CQDs prepared from different biomass carbon sources [6,59–62]. However, this is the first time N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from fruit peels were modified with l-cysteine (through an amide coupling reaction) to be applied in Hg2+ detection by fluorescence quenching. l-cysteine ligand has an advantage on selectivity given the high affinity between Hg2+ and sulfur atoms on NCQDs surface. In this context, we evaluated the effect on fluorescence intensity of NCQDs/CYS generated by other metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, As3+), as shown in Fig. 10C. Hg2+ induced strongly quenching fluorescence, while other cations had no significant effects, except Co2+ and Cu2+ ions, which slightly reduced PL intensity.

These selectivity results follow Pearson's hard and soft acids and bases (HSAB) theory [63,64]. Soft acids prefer soft bases to form stable covalent complexes, while hard acids prefer to bind hard bases. Table 1 shows the absolute hardness of cations (η), which is higher for hard acids and low for soft acids [63,64]. l-cysteine represents a soft Lewis base with a highly polarizable R-S- group, with more affinity for soft Hg2+ than hard acids. This affinity induces the formation of a chemical bond between the thiolate group of NCQD/CYS and Hg2+, producing PL quenching, as is shown in Fig. 11 .

Other CQDs-based metal ions sensors prepared from biomass and without post-synthesis modification are superficially rich in oxygen-containing and NH2 groups, which are hard bases and could affect the Hg2+ selectivity [47,62,65,66]. Comparing the cation's hardness and the basicity of ligands or functional groups on CQDs surface are significant features in optimizing fluorescent detection of metal ions [47] to maintain the advantages of these materials obtained from biomass resources (high-value, inexpensive, renewable, and environmentally friendly).

Fluorescence lifetime measurements were developed to understand better the role of Hg2+on the PL properties of NCQDs/CYS (Fig. 12 A). FL decays are fitted with a biexponential function (Eq. (3)), and the average fluorescence lifetime (τFL) is calculated with Eq. (4). Inset in Fig. 12A shows the PL lifetimes and fitting parameters calculated with the PL decay curves.
(3)
y =
A 1
e x
p
(
− x
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e x
p
(
− x
τ 2
)
+
y 0
(4)
τ FL
=
∑ i
A i
τ i 2
∑ i
A i
τ i

The decay curves reflect two distinct processes of excitonic relaxation: a fast component (τ1) associated with intrinsic emission states of the graphite core [67,68] and a slow component (τ2) related to the exciton recombination through surface trap states and could indicate a surface rich in functional groups. The average PL lifetimes of the NCQDs/CYS is 5.0 ns, concordant with similar systems [69] [70]. The PL decays of NCQDs/CYS in the presence of Hg2+ ions remained almost unchanged, suggesting a static quenching that involves the formation of a nonfluorescent ground-state complex due to the coordination between NCQDs/CYS and Hg2+, which led to the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs [71,72]. The formation of this nonfluorescent complex is also evidenced by the changes in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NCQD/CYS (Fig. 12B) since the absorption bands decreased in presence of Hg2+ (compared to the UV–Vis spectrum of NCQDs/CYS) due to bonding between NCQDs/CYS and Hg2+ hypochromic effect) [56,72]. The unchanged average fluorescence lifetimes and changes in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCQDs in presence of Hg2+ feature a static quenching mechanism [72,73].

To confirm the coordination of Hg2+ and NCQDs/CYS [56,72], we compare the normalized FT-IR spectra of NCQDs/CYS with the sample containing Hg2+ (Fig. 12C). A significant reduction in the intensity at 2560 cm−1 of R-S-H (thiol group) is produced due to the coordination of Hg2+ ions to l-cysteine through R-S − (thiolate group). Due to its high affinity, we recall that Hg2+ ions (soft acid) coordinate to NCQDs/CYS mainly through l-cysteine's thiolate (soft base). In addition, we observe a broadening of the band at 601 cm−1 (C-S). The coordination of a heavy atom such as mercury to the thiolate group gives rigidity to the bond (C-S), generating a decrease in the vibration time (Δt) and a broadening of its energy ΔE (Heisenberg uncertainty principle), which could explain the corresponding broadening of its absorption band, corroborating the coordination of mercury ions to the thiolate group [74].

Fig. 13 shows a simplified scheme proposing the mechanism for detecting mercury ions by NCQDs/CYS. As can be seen, a nonfluorescent coordination complex is formed between mercury ions and l-cysteine of NCQDs/CYS, according to a static quenching mechanism.

This work reports the first use of wastes of peach palm (Bactris gasipaes) peels to obtain NCQDS with the capability to be applied in fluorescent detection of Hg2+ ions. Synthesis of biomass-derived NCQDs induced with microwave irradiation produced semi-crystalline particles with sizes of 3.28 nm. These nanomaterials have a core made of graphite and a surface covered in amino and carboxylic groups and other groups that contain oxygen. The surface of the NCQDs was appropriately functionalized with l-cysteine by an amide coupling between the carboxyl groups of the NCQDs and the amine group of l-cysteine. The N-doped graphitic core was preserved, and l-cysteine ligand was incorporated on the surface. Likewise, the nanoparticle size was kept (3.48 nm). Despite the complexity of the surface, we identified the XPS and FT-IR spectral changes and evidenced a chemical bonding formation between l-cysteine and NCQDs. In addition, fluorescence quantum yield was increased from 20.5 % (for NCQDs) to 25.4 % for NCQDs/CYS.

NCQDs/CYS applied to detection of mercury ions by quenching of fluorescence showed exciting results. We found a sensitivity of 4.10×104 M −1 and LOD of 0.19 μM. Maybe the most essential feature of NCQDs/CYS was selectivity to mercury ions. The strategic functionalization with l-cysteine of the NCQDs provided a new and efficient material with good sensitivity and high selectivity to detect Hg2+. Fluorescence decay measurement evidenced the formation of a nonfluorescent coordination complex via a static quenching mechanism. The results demonstrate that NCQDs/CYS nanoparticles are excellent candidates for rapid and economical quantitative fluorescent detection of Hg2+ ions in aqueous medium, which can be applied mainly in regions vulnerable to mercury pollution, such as Colombia.

Brayan Stiven Gómez Pineros: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gilma Granados-Oliveros: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Nuevos Materiales Nano y Supramoleculares research group at the Department of Chemistry, Universidad Nacional de Colombia, and Professeur Sebastian Bello of Photochemistry Laboratory, Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro for Time-resolved fluorescence measurements.
